headline: Fully permissionless contracts with only passive restricted-jurisdiction clauses on the frontend.

findings.text: Morpho Blue's core contracts do not use access control lists, whitelists, or KYC modifiers for user-facing actions such as deposits, borrows, or market creation.
Admission of user actions (supplying, borrowing) does not require approval from any off-chain operator, sequencer, or permissioned committee.
Morpho's Terms of Use include boilerplate clauses (A3-passive) restricting usage from certain jurisdictions and prohibiting VPN circumvention.
The protocol actively documents and provides software development kits (SDKs) to facilitate independent, direct-contract access and the creation of third-party user interfaces (A3b-ii).
The protocol's smart contracts do not incorporate on-chain sanctions blocklists or OFAC screening mechanisms.
Read access (state visibility) and write access (depositing and borrowing) are both completely permissionless at the smart contract level.

The official Terms of Use state: 'Important notice: no Services (as defined below) are offered to persons or entities who reside in, are citizens of, are located in, are incorporated in, or have a registered office in a Restricted Territory (as defined below).'

steelman.red: The protocol's frontend terms of service explicitly prohibit access from restricted jurisdictions and explicitly ban VPN use to circumvent these rules, theoretically barring access for a large class of users.
steelman.orange: The strictness of the Terms of Service against restricted territories implies strong intent from the Morpho Association to deter users in certain jurisdictions from the official interface.
steelman.green: The core lending contracts are fully immutable and permissionless without any on-chain whitelists or operator gates, and the official frontend relies solely on passive ToS clauses without enforcing active KYC walls, wallet screening, or IP geo-blocking.

rationale.verdict: Choosing green because the passive restricted-jurisdiction and VPN-prohibition clauses in the frontend's Terms of Use do not translate to active technical enforcement (A3-active) or contract-level gating (A1), leaving admission permissionless in practice.

evidence.shows: Verbatim restricted territory and VPN prohibition clauses (A3-passive, A6).
Deployed Morpho Blue core contract is fully permissionless for deposits and borrows.
Documentation and guidance for building third-party 'Earn & Borrow' interfaces directly on the contracts.
Canonical directory of deployed Morpho contract, token, and governance multisig addresses.

unknowns: A3: could not verify active frontend IP geoblocking or wallet-address screening (A3-active) from static fetch

protocol_metadata.about: Morpho is a decentralized lending protocol that provides a primitive base layer for permissionless, isolated variable-rate markets. Users can lend or borrow assets directly in isolated pools (Morpho Blue) or passively allocate capital via curated yield optimizers (Morpho Vaults).

headline: No contract-level allowlists or on-chain sanctions checks; core protocol fully permissionless; ToS restrictions are passive self-attestation only

findings.text: The deployed Morpho.sol at 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb (Ethereum) contains no whitelist/allowlist modifiers on any user-facing function. The supply(), withdraw(), borrow(), repay(), supplyCollateral(), withdrawCollateral(), liquidate(), and flashLoan() functions require no role or KYC gate — the only checks are that the market exists, the caller is authorized by the position owner (isAuthorized mapping, which any user controls for their own account), and that the position is healthy. The full source code shown on morpho.org homepage and verified against the etherscan-confirmed address confirms zero access control modifiers on user entry/exit paths.

No off-chain operator approval is needed to admit any user action. deposit/supply, borrow, repay, withdrawCollateral, and withdraw are all admitted unconditionally by the contract. The onlyOwner modifier is used only for admin functions (enableIrm, enableLltv, setFee, setFeeRecipient, setOwner) that affect protocol configuration, not user admission. Liquidation and flashLoan are also permissionless. No keeper, sequencer, or privileged relayer appears in the admission path for any user-facing function class.

The official frontend at app.morpho.org (linked from morpho.org) was not directly fetchable to confirm a block banner, but the morpho.org homepage rendered fully without any geo-block or KYC wall. The ToS (fetched from morpho.org/terms-of-use, version dated November 3, 2025) states in the Important notice: 'no Services are offered to persons or entities who reside in, are citizens of, are located in, are incorporated in, or have a registered office in a Restricted Territory' and 'Use of a virtual private network (VPN) to circumvent the restrictions set forth herein is strictly prohibited.' Section 21 (Eligibility) requires users to self-certify they are not in restricted territories or on sanctions lists. Section 17 lists grounds for restricting access including being on OFAC SDN lists or in Restricted Territories (Ivory Coast, Cuba, Belarus, Iran, Iraq, Liberia, North Korea, Russia, Sudan, Syria). These are A3-passive clauses: they describe legal policy and self-certification, not a demonstrated runtime enforcement mechanism. No active IP geo-block, wallet-address screening, or KYC wall was observed in the fetched homepage content.

A3b-i: No IPFS-pinned official frontend CID was found in this run. The ToS definition of 'App' includes 'all applications accessible through Morpho's different domains and subdomains (Morpho.org, Morpho.dev)' — any such mirrors would share the same ToS. A3b-ii: Multiple independent access paths exist and are documented by Morpho itself: (1) a public SDK at docs.morpho.org/sdks/introduction/ for direct contract interaction; (2) a public API at docs.morpho.org/api/morpho/; (3) direct contract interaction via the deployed address 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb, fully open-source and permissionless; (4) the Morpho GitHub (github.com/morpho-org) contains all contract source. These A3b-ii paths are not bound by the frontend ToS for direct contract calls and are documented by the protocol.

No on-chain sanctions blocklist or address screening module was found in the core Morpho.sol contract at 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb. The isAuthorized mapping is user-controlled (for position delegation), not an admin-controlled access list. There is no integration with Chainalysis, TRM, or any on-chain blocklist visible in the contract code shown on the morpho.org homepage or in the etherscan search results.

Read access: fully permissionless. All state variables (position(), market(), isIrmEnabled(), isLltvEnabled(), idToMarketParams()) are public mappings callable by anyone. Write access: also permissionless for user-facing functions. supply(), borrow(), repay(), supplyCollateral(), withdrawCollateral(), liquidate(), and flashLoan() all accept calls from any address. withdraw() and borrowing on behalf of another require only that msg.sender == onBehalf or isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender] == true — a condition the position owner controls for their own account.

ToS verbatim quotes obtained from morpho.org/terms-of-use (version November 3, 2025): Important notice clause: 'no Services (as defined below) are offered to persons or entities who reside in, are citizens of, are located in, are incorporated in, or have a registered office in a Restricted Territory (as defined below). Use of a virtual private network (VPN) to circumvent the restrictions set forth herein is strictly prohibited.' Eligibility clause (Section 21): 'You are not located, organized, or resident in a country or territory that is, or whose government is, the subject of sanctions, including but not limited to the Restricted Territories.' Restricted Territories listed (Section 17): 'Ivory Coast, Cuba, Belarus, Iran, Iraq, Liberia, North Korea, Russia, Sudan, and Syria.' These are passive self-certification clauses; no runtime enforcement mechanism is described or was observed.

steelman.red: The ToS grants Morpho Association unilateral right to restrict access at any time (Section 17) including for sanctions-listed wallets, and if the frontend were implementing silent wallet-address screening via TRM or Chainalysis this would constitute a contract-independent but admission-relevant gatekeeping mechanism — though no evidence of active enforcement was found in this run.
steelman.orange: The official frontend ToS prohibits use by residents of roughly 10 sanctioned countries and explicitly bans VPN circumvention; if the official frontend actively IP-blocks those regions (which could not be confirmed or ruled out from a static fetch), and no equivalently-accessible A3b-ii alternative is surfaced to non-technical users, an orange grade would be warranted.
steelman.green: The core Morpho.sol contracts at 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb are fully permissionless with zero access-control modifiers on user entry/exit functions, no on-chain sanctions list, and independent A3b-ii paths (SDK, API, direct contract) are publicly documented — all of which point to green regardless of frontend ToS boilerplate.

rationale.verdict: Choosing green because the core contracts (verified at 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb on Ethereum) contain no whitelist, KYC, or sanctions-check modifiers on any user-facing function; no active frontend enforcement (IP block, wallet screening, KYC wall) was observed in the fetched homepage; the ToS restrictions are A3-passive self-certification boilerplate; and multiple A3b-ii alternative access paths (SDK, API, direct contract interaction) are officially documented. The orange steel-man requires confirmed A3-active enforcement evidence (type a–d per rubric), which was not found.

evidence.shows: Morpho Blue V1 core contract on Ethereum mainnet — confirmed deployed address, no proxy, non-upgradeable

Homepage rendering with full Morpho.sol source code showing no allowlist/whitelist modifiers on supply, withdraw, borrow, repay, supplyCollateral, withdrawCollateral, liquidate, flashLoan functions; confirms security contact security@morpho.org; confirms Bug Bounty on Cantina; confirms auditors (Cantina, ChainSecurity, OpenZeppelin, Spearbit, Trail of Bits, Certora)

ToS version November 3, 2025: passive restricted-territories self-certification clause (Ivory Coast, Cuba, Belarus, Iran, Iraq, Liberia, North Korea, Russia, Sudan, Syria), VPN prohibition, OFAC eligibility self-attestation in Section 21 — all passive policy, no described runtime enforcement mechanism
Official deployed contract addresses per chain; Morpho V1 Ethereum address confirmed as 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb; documents SDK and API as independent access paths
Full audit list including Spearbit, ChainSecurity, Certora, Blackthorn, Zellic, Cantina contest covering Morpho V1 and V2 contracts; confirms extensive security review coverage
Morpho bug bounty program hosted on Cantina
Morpho governance forum (referenced in ToS and homepage as governance discussion venue)
MORPHO ERC-20 governance token on Ethereum mainnet at 0x58d97b57bb95320f9a05dc918aef65434969c2b2

unknowns: A3-active: Could not confirm or rule out runtime IP geo-blocking on app.morpho.org via direct fetch (homepage at morpho.org rendered without block; direct app subdomain fetch was not attempted in this run). No block banner, HTTP 451, or named third-party screening provider (Chainalysis/TRM script tag) was observed in fetched content, but a full browser-rendered check of app.morpho.org was not performed.
A3b-i: No IPFS CID for an official pinned frontend release was found in this run; could not determine whether an IPFS mirror exists.

admin_addresses.role: Morpho Blue V1 core protocol (owner field governs IRM/LLTV enablement and fees; owner address not resolved in this run)

protocol_metadata.about: Morpho is a permissionless, non-custodial lending protocol that lets anyone supply or borrow crypto assets against collateral in isolated markets defined by (loan token, collateral token, oracle, IRM, LLTV) tuples. Users interact directly with immutable smart contracts; there are no intermediaries or custodians. On top of the core protocol, MetaMorpho/Vault V2 contracts allow risk managers (Curators) to create ERC-4626 vaults that automatically allocate deposits across multiple markets. The protocol is governed by MORPHO token holders through the Morpho DAO.

headline: Permissionless on-chain admission with passive ToS only and documented direct/third-party alternatives
short_headline: No user whitelists; direct contract access documented

findings.text: No whitelist/allowlist modifiers or user-address checks on core user-facing entry/exit functions (supply, borrow, repay, withdraw, liquidate, createMarket, supplyCollateral, withdrawCollateral) in the deployed Morpho contract. 'not whitelisted' error string applies only to un-enabled IRM/LLTV parameters by the protocol owner; user admission is unconditional once market params are valid. Owner-only functions (enableIrm, enableLltv, setFee, setOwner) gate risk parameters, not users.

No off-chain operators, keepers, sequencers, or privileged relayers required to admit any user action. All core functions are direct on-chain public/external calls with no approval step. Oracles/liquidation bots affect liveness and health-factor enforcement but not admission of new deposits/borrows (explicitly documented as permissionless primitive).

Official frontend (app.morpho.org) shows no A3-active enforcement: no geo-blocking banners, no 'not available in your jurisdiction' blocks, no wallet-address sanctions screening (Chainalysis/TRM/etc.), no KYC wall, and no runtime rejection. Only prominent 'Connect' wallet prompt. ToS contains only A3-passive boilerplate (self-certification of non-sanctioned status, Restricted Territories list, VPN-circumvention prohibition).

Strong A3b-ii independent paths documented and operational: (1) direct contract interaction via Etherscan/explorers (explicitly recommended in docs for when app inaccessible); (2) fallback.morpho.org emergency UI (resilient, same-team but separate deployment, prioritizes exit); (3) third-party frontends (Oku.trade Morpho integration, World App Morpho Mini App, curator.morpho.org). These are not bound by the Morpho Association Apps ToS and enable full on-chain tx submission without official frontend.

No on-chain sanctions/OFAC blocklist or address screening in the Morpho contract or any periphery. No updatable user blocklist controlled by single party. All sanctions language is off-chain ToS self-attestation only (no on-chain enforcement visible in verified source or Etherscan).

Read access fully permissionless: any address can query markets, positions, health factors, oracles via RPC or block explorers with no gating. Write access (deposit/supply, borrow, repay, withdraw, liquidate) also permissionless on-chain for any EOA/contract with token balances/approvals; no role-based or address-list gating on entry/exit.

ToS located at https://morpho.org/terms-of-use/ (version dated November 3, 2025). Verbatim eligibility clause: 'You are not subject of any sanctions administered or enforced by ... OFAC ... You are not located, organized, or resident in a country or territory that is ... Restricted Territories (Ivory Coast, Cuba, Belarus, Iran, Iraq, Liberia, North Korea, Russia, Sudan, and Syria) ... Use of a virtual private network (“VPN”) to circumvent the restrictions set forth herein is strictly prohibited.' Services explicitly defined as 'the Apps' (web/API interfaces); 'the permissionless Morpho Protocols' are distinguished as the on-chain layer. No KYC requirement stated.

steelman.red: The ToS prohibits residents of nine listed countries and reserves the right to revoke app access for suspected breaches, creating a de-facto off-chain gate that could chill usage even if on-chain remains open.
steelman.orange: Official frontend relies on passive ToS self-certification with VPN prohibition and no visible active enforcement, but non-technical users may still face friction without the main UI, and alternatives like direct contract interaction require technical sophistication.
steelman.green: Core on-chain protocol admits every user unconditionally via public functions with zero user whitelists, no operator approval gates, and no on-chain blocklists; frontend applies only standard passive ToS boilerplate with no runtime A3-active blocks; multiple independent A3b-ii paths (direct contract, fallback UI, third-party frontends) are officially documented and live, meeting all green criteria.

rationale.verdict: Choosing green because the deployed immutable Morpho contract (Etherscan 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb, source at GitHub commit 1478e9cfe1b4d514f80682b3b60e4e12ff3ee45a) has no user-facing whitelist modifiers or admission gates on any entry/exit function, the official app.morpho.org frontend shows zero active enforcement (no banners, screening, or KYC), ToS restrictions are passive self-attestation only, and docs explicitly document robust independent alternatives including direct explorer interaction and third-party UIs that bypass the Apps entirely.

evidence.shows: Deployed immutable Morpho singleton on Ethereum; owner 0x937Ce2d6c488b361825D2DB5e8A70e26d48afEd5; ABI confirms supply/borrow/repay/withdraw/liquidate/createMarket are external with no user-address whitelist checks; 'not whitelisted' error applies only to IRM/LLTV parameters; no on-chain user blocklist or sanctions oracle.

Source of permissionless lending primitive; core Morpho.sol has no user allowlist/onlyWhitelisted modifiers on entry functions; market creation and supply/borrow are open once owner-enabled params are used; governance-minimized design emphasized.

Full ToS (Nov 3, 2025): verbatim Restricted Territories list (Ivory Coast, Cuba, Belarus, Iran, Iraq, Liberia, North Korea, Russia, Sudan, Syria), sanctions self-warranty, VPN prohibition, 'no Services offered to Restricted Territory persons'; Services defined as Apps only; protocol layer called 'permissionless Morpho Protocols'; no KYC or active screening mechanism described; enforcement limited to app-access revocation at sole discretion.

Live frontend: no jurisdiction banners, no geo-redirects, no KYC wall, no wallet sanctions screening, no rendering blocks; only 'Connect' wallet prompt visible; fully loads without any active restriction UI.

Explicitly documents fallback.morpho.org (emergency resilient UI), direct contract interaction via explorers/Etherscan, and third-party alternatives as primary options 'if you don't want to use the Main Morpho app or if you are not able to access it'; confirms on-chain protocol remains accessible independently of official Apps.

Canonical index of all deployed contracts (Morpho 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb on Ethereum and equivalents on other chains); confirms immutable singletons and periphery; no mention of user whitelists or admission contracts.
Homepage describes open infrastructure for lending/borrowing with no eligibility gates or KYC mentions; links to docs and support; emphasizes 'universal lending network' and 'open infrastructure'.
Governance discussion confirming Morpho DAO/multisig ownership transfer on Base (0xeC5e4a3cE93c284441dC6a2Dd5F0D46a202F00a3); illustrates on-chain governance path for privileged roles.

protocol_metadata.about: Morpho is a permissionless non-custodial lending protocol on EVM chains (Ethereum, Base, and 30+ others) that lets any user supply assets to earn yield or borrow against collateral in isolated markets with owner-enabled but publicly usable risk parameters (LLTV, IRM). It uses a gas-efficient singleton architecture with no user-level gates, KYC, or intermediaries; governance occurs via MORPHO token and multisig with documented DAO transition path.

headline: Morpho core markets admit users through public contract calls; only passive frontend ToS restrictions were verified.

findings.text: I found no user-facing whitelist, KYC, allowlist, onlyWhitelisted, isKYCed, or onlyRole-style admission gate in the deployed Morpho V1 core ABI or displayed Morpho.sol source. The only privileged owner-gated functions in the core source are configuration functions such as setOwner, enableIrm, enableLltv, setFee, and setFeeRecipient; user-facing createMarket, supply, withdraw, borrow, repay, supplyCollateral, withdrawCollateral, liquidate, and flashLoan are exposed as external functions without owner-only admission.

Core user action placement does not require approval by an off-chain keeper, sequencer, relayer, or oracle poster: supply and supplyCollateral accept public calls for existing markets; withdraw, borrow, and withdrawCollateral require the caller to be the position owner or an address authorized by that owner; repay is public for a position; liquidation is public when health conditions fail. Borrow, withdrawCollateral, and liquidation depend on the market oracle for health checks, but that is a market-liveness dependency, not an off-chain admission approval path.

The official Terms of Use contain passive eligibility, sanctions, restricted-territory, and VPN-circumvention restrictions: no Services are offered to persons in Restricted Territories; users represent they are not sanctioned, not on sanctions lists, and not located, organized, or resident in sanctioned territories.

I loaded the official app URL via static fetch and did not observe a verbatim block banner, HTTP 451 text, KYC wall text, or named screening-provider rejection message in the fetched content. I therefore did not verify active frontend enforcement; the verified frontend restriction evidence is passive ToS language only.

Morpho documents independent alternative paths not limited to the main hosted app: the docs show market creation via Etherscan on the deployed Morpho contract; the SDK docs describe packages for fetching/simulating user interactions and building integrations; Morpho Lite and Fallback are open-source frontends that can be run locally, self-hosted, or white-labeled, with Fallback supporting withdraw, repay, add collateral, and remove collateral without additional infrastructure/services.

I found no onchain sanctions or compliance blocklist in the verified core contract source or deployed ABI: storage consists of owner, feeRecipient, positions, markets, enabled IRMs, enabled LLTVs, per-user authorizations, nonces, and idToMarketParams, and no OFAC/TRM/Chainalysis/blocklist mapping or enforcement function appears in the inspected core contract.

Read access is public through exposed view functions such as owner, feeRecipient, isAuthorized, isIrmEnabled, isLltvEnabled, market, position, idToMarketParams, and extSloads. Write access to core lending functions is public subject to ordinary position ownership or user-delegated authorization for actions that modify an existing position on behalf of another address.

The ToS/Legal page was located and the relevant clause is: "no Services (as defined below) are offered to persons or entities who reside in, are citizens of, are located in, are incorporated in, or have a registered office in a Restricted Territory (as defined below). Use of a virtual private network (\"VPN\") to circumvent the restrictions set forth herein is strictly prohibited."

steelman.red: The strongest red argument is that the official ToS reserves a right to deny Services to sanctioned or restricted users and the protocol has governance-controlled configuration over enabled IRMs, LLTVs, and fees, but the evidence does not show contract-level user whitelists, KYC, or single-operator admission approval.
steelman.orange: The strongest orange argument is that the official interface is legally restricted by passive ToS language and the main SPA could theoretically enforce restrictions at runtime that were not visible in static fetches, but I found no qualifying A3-active evidence such as a block banner, KYC wall, wallet-screening provider, or HTTP 451 response.
steelman.green: The strongest green argument is that deployed core contracts expose public user actions without whitelist/KYC/blocklist gates, Morpho documents direct Etherscan and SDK access plus open-source fallback/lite frontends, and the only verified frontend restriction is passive ToS language.

rationale.verdict: Choosing green because the concrete contract evidence shows public user-facing entry and exit functions without whitelist/KYC/blocklist admission gates, the only authorization checks are user-owned or user-delegated position controls, and the verified frontend restrictions are passive ToS clauses rather than proven runtime enforcement.

evidence.shows: Canonical deployed contract address list: Ethereum Morpho V1 core at 0xBBBBBbbBBb9cC5e90e3b3Af64bdAF62C37EEFFCb, V2 factory addresses, source-code repo links, and MORPHO token address references.

Block explorer page for the deployed Ethereum Morpho core contract; Etherscan reports source code verified exact match and exposes the ABI including public write functions borrow, createMarket, repay, setAuthorization, supply, supplyCollateral, withdraw, withdrawCollateral, liquidate, and flashLoan.

Official site describes Morpho as open infrastructure and embeds Morpho V1 core source. The displayed source shows storage mappings for owner, feeRecipient, position, market, isIrmEnabled, isLltvEnabled, isAuthorized, nonce, and idToMarketParams; owner-only functions configure owner, IRMs, LLTVs, fees, and fee recipient; user-facing supply, withdraw, borrow, repay, supplyCollateral, withdrawCollateral, liquidate, flashLoan, and per-user authorization functions are external.

Tagged Morpho V1 core source: supply and supplyCollateral require existing market and nonzero addresses/assets, not whitelist/KYC; withdraw, borrow, and withdrawCollateral require _isSenderAuthorized(onBehalf); _isSenderAuthorized returns true for msg.sender == onBehalf or a per-user authorization; no sanctions/blocklist mapping appears in the inspected storage or user-facing functions.

Interface comments define user-facing functions and note market dependency assumptions. The interface states anyone is authorized to modify their own positions, while managing another user's position requires authorization from that user; oracle or IRM failures may affect liveness but are listed as dependency risks rather than admission approval requirements.

Morpho docs describe a variable rate market as an isolated immutable lending pool with one collateral asset and one loan asset, and state that creating a Morpho Market is permissionless and does not require a governance vote once parameters are available.
Docs describe direct contract access for market creation via Etherscan on the Morpho contract, including using createMarket and the Ethereum mainnet Morpho address.

Official ToS defines hosted Apps and Services, states restricted-territory and VPN-circumvention restrictions, includes sanctions and eligibility representations, and reserves the right to deny access to Services for sanctions or unlawful-use reasons. These are legal/ToS restrictions, not evidence of onchain enforcement.

Official app URL was fetched. The static fetch produced no extractable body text, and no block-banner text, HTTP 451 text, KYC wall text, wallet-screening rejection text, or named screening-provider message was visible in the fetched result.

Morpho states the Interface should let the user do and see what the smart contract exposes, should be fully open-source with SDKs for specialized apps, should be accessible on IPFS, downloadable, self-hostable or operable on desktop, and should allow any user to interact with all Morpho Blue smart-contract capacities without needing to be technical.

Open-source Morpho Lite/Fallback monorepo. README states Fallback can view deposits and borrow positions, withdraw from MetaMorpho vaults, repay loans, add collateral, remove collateral, support any chain with Morpho contracts, and requires no additional infrastructure/services; Lite adds exploration, deposit, and open-position flows.

Morpho Lite blog states Lite is a simple open-source version of the Morpho App; core features are Earn deposit/withdraw and Borrow supply collateral/borrow/repay; it can be used to deploy white-labeled or customized instances with configurable RPC settings, listing policies, and chain support.

SDK docs list packages for Morpho market, vault, and position entities, simulation of user interactions like supplying, borrowing, or depositing before execution, viem live-chain fetching, liquidity calculations, liquidation bots, React hooks, and GraphQL API access.

Governance docs state MORPHO token-holders vote through Snapshot, approved actions are implemented by a 5/9 governance multisig, and list morpho.eth at 0xcBa28b38103307Ec8dA98377ffF9816C164f9AFa on Ethereum and Base.
Docs identify MORPHO as the governance token and state voting power is based on MORPHO token holdings.
Etherscan token page for MORPHO ERC-20 at 0x58d97b57bb95320f9a05dc918aef65434969c2b2.

Morpho audit page lists professional security reviews and contests including Morpho V1 reviews by OpenZeppelin, Spearbit, and Cantina; Vaults V2 reviews by Spearbit, ChainSecurity, Blackthorn, Certora, Zellic, and Cantina; and states Morpho uses formal verification, mutation tests, fuzzing, unit tests, peer reviews, external reviews, contests, and bounties.

protocol_metadata.about: Morpho is lending infrastructure built around isolated markets and vaults. Users can supply assets, borrow against collateral, repay, withdraw, or deposit into curated vaults, while builders and curators can create markets and vault strategies on top of the same underlying contracts. Its distinctive design is permissionless market creation with immutable market parameters, externalized risk curation through vaults, and SDK/front-end infrastructure for third-party lending applications.
